title: The SMR Data Center Promise: Why Nuclear Reality Doesn't Match the Marketing Timeline
text:
The SMR Data Center Promise: Why Nuclear Reality Doesn't Match the Marketing Timeline
A Reality Check on Small Modular Reactors, HALEU Fuel, and the Race to Power AI Data Center
If you've been following the AI infrastructure boom, you've seen the headlines: Amazon commits to 5 GW of SMR capacity. Google signs first U.S. corporate PPA for an advanced reactor. Oracle announces a gigawatt-scale data center powered by three small modular reactors, with 'permits secured.' Microsoft, Meta, and every hyperscaler are racing to lock in nuclear power purchase agreements.
The message is clear: Small modular reactors will solve the AI power crisis. Clean, safe, abundant nuclear energy, ready when you need it.
There's just one problem: GPUs move in 2-3 year cycles. Reactors move in decades. Right now, Wall Street is pretending those curves intersect in a way that works for your balance sheet.
I spent my early career running nuclear reactors on submarines, then built data centers at scale for Oracle, AWS, and Meta. I know when marketing is getting ahead of physics and supply chains.
Nuclear power is one of the safest, most reliable ways to generate electricity. But it is not fast. And the current wave of SMR promises is setting up nuclear power for another credibility crisis, when we need it most.
Timeline Reality Check (TL;DR)
Compare these timelines to your GPU refresh budget.
Near-Term (2025-2030):
          
      
        
    
Medium-Term (2030-2040):
          
      
        
    
Long-Term (2035-2050):
          
      
        
    
Bottom line: If you need new baseload power before 2030, nuclear isn't the answer unless you're already well into a restart with financing and a signed PPA. For anything you start evaluating in 2025, think late-2020s at best for restarts, and 2030s+ for new-build SMRs.
The Three Paths to Nuclear Power: A Timeline Reality Check
Let's talk about what's possible and in what timeframe:
Option 1: Restart Existing Reactors (2.5-4 Years with Financing Secured)
This is the fastest option, and it's quicker than most people realize, once financing and PPAs are in place.
Restarting decommissioned nuclear plants is the quickest path to nuclear-powered data centers. These facilities have existing infrastructure, known technology, and proven designs.
Actual restart timelines (decision to operational):
          
      
        
    
The key insight: These 2.5-4 year timelines assume the plant is in good material condition, financing is secured (including federal loan support), and a creditworthy offtaker has signed a long-term PPA. If you're starting a restart evaluation from scratch in 2025 without a committed buyer, add 1-2 years for due diligence, financing, and PPA negotiations before the regulatory clock even starts.
The transmission bottleneck:
Even if a reactor comes online in 3 years, that doesn't mean your data center gets power. Unless you're building on-site (behind the meter), you'll enter the interconnection queue, which is currently 4-7 years for large projects.
Critical point: This queue exists regardless of whether your power source is nuclear, wind, solar, or gas. The grid doesn't care what's generating the electrons; substations need upgrades, transmission lines need permitting, and everyone's waiting in the same line. This is why 'behind the meter' is the only actual shortcut, and why co-location near existing nuclear sites matters so much.
Option 2: Build Gen III+ Reactors (8-14+ Years)
This is what happened in the U.S. recently.
The Vogtle Units 3 and 4 AP1000 reactors were supposed to demonstrate that modern Gen III+ reactors could be built on time and on budget:
          
      
        
    
These are the first new U.S. nuclear reactors built in over 30 years. They use the most modern Gen III+ passive safety technology. Experienced contractors built them at an existing nuclear site with all infrastructure in place. And they still took more than a decade.
In China, AP1000 reactors took an average of 9 years, not the 4-5 years predicted. The 'four-year construction timeline' that nuclear vendors love to quote? It happened once, in Japan, in 1997, with the ABWR at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa. It has not been replicated at scale anywhere in the Western world since.
China is proving it's physically possible: China's HTR-PM at Shidaowan reached full commercial power in December 2023. It's a working, operating Gen IV SMR producing power today. But this underscores that China's nuclear supply chain is decades ahead of the West's…they have the factories, the trained workforce, the regulatory experience, and the political will to move fast, all of which the U.S. and Europe are still trying to rebuild after four decades of not building reactors.
Option 3: Deploy Small Modular Reactors (2030-2040... Maybe)
This is where marketing dramatically outpaces reality.
Here's what the data center-SMR announcements promise:
Here's the reality of current SMR programs:
NuScale Power (the only NRC-certified SMR in the U.S.):
          
      
        
    
TerraPower Natrium (Bill Gates-backed, 345 MW):
          
      
        
    
Ontario BWRX-300 (GE Hitachi, Canada):
          
      
        
    
Most serious analysts now expect first commercial SMR deployments in the early-to-mid-2030s for Gen III+ light-water designs, typically with 1-3 year delays and significant cost overruns. Advanced (Gen IV) SMRs face 'more substantial delays' with factory manufacturing 'more likely to be available by around 2040.'
The HALEU Problem: The Fuel That Doesn't Exist at Scale
Here's the part that should terrify anyone counting on advanced reactors for near-term power needs: High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium (HALEU) is not currently available from domestic suppliers at a commercial scale.
Critical distinction: Not all SMRs face this problem.
Gen III+ Light Water SMRs (such as NuScale's VOYGR and GE Hitachi's BWRX-300) use standard Low-Enriched Uranium (LEU, <5% enrichment), the same fuel as conventional reactors. These designs face supply chain constraints and first-of-a-kind project challenges, but they're not waiting on HALEU.
Gen IV Advanced Reactors (such as TerraPower's Natrium, X-energy's Xe-100, and Kairos Power's fluoride-salt-cooled designs) require HALEU (5-20% enriched uranium) to achieve their promised smaller designs, longer operating cycles, and increased efficiency.
If your SMR press release mentions 'advanced reactor' or 'Gen IV,' odds are you're betting on HALEU. And HALEU doesn't exist at a commercial scale.
The supply-demand gap:
Before Russia invaded Ukraine, Russia was the only commercial source of HALEU fuel. That source is now gone. The U.S. is scrambling to build domestic enrichment capacity, but even with aggressive investment, we're looking at 6 MT/year from a single whole cascade, vs. 40+ MT needed by 2030.
Real-world impact: TerraPower's Natrium reactor was delayed by at least two years specifically because 'there will not be sufficient commercial capacity to manufacture HALEU fuel' to meet the 2028 target. They've since secured supply from South Africa - because U.S. capacity doesn't exist - and the target is now 2031.
What the Hyperscalers Are Actually Buying
Let's be clear about what these data center-SMR agreements represent:
The disconnect:
          
      
        
    
Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs) are not construction contracts. They are letters of intent to explore partnerships. They contain no firm delivery dates, no penalty clauses for delays, and often no binding financial commitments.
Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) for SMRs dated 2030-2035+ are essentially bets that:
1. SMR technology will be commercially deployable by then
2. Regulatory approvals will be granted
3. Supply chains (especially HALEU) will exist
4. First-of-a-kind projects won't face typical cost overruns
5. Someone else will solve the complex problems first
Critical distinction: Most hyperscaler SMR deals are grid-level PPAs, not behind-the-meter power. Google's Kairos deal supplies power into the TVA grid - it's not physically wired to a single Google campus. These PPAs de-risk clean energy supply in the regional power mix and provide revenue certainty for reactor developers. Still, they don't solve local substation capacity, transmission congestion, or interconnection queues.
A PPA gives you a financial hedge and a clean energy credit. It doesn't give you a dedicated wire from the reactor to your racks.
The Factory That Doesn't Exist Yet
SMR vendors love to talk about 'factory fabrication' and 'modular construction' as game-changers for cost and schedule. They're right - in theory.
The problem: We don't have the factories.
SMRs only become economically competitive at 'Nth-of-a-kind' production - meaning somewhere around unit 10-20, after the learning curve has been captured. Right now, we're building units 1-5: bespoke, hand-crafted, artisan nuclear reactors with all the first-of-a-kind challenges that come with them.
We're trying to build the iPhone of nuclear power, but we haven't built the factory to make it yet. We're hand-soldering prototypes in a lab while promising customers they can buy them at the Apple Store next year.
The actual path forward: First wave of SMRs (2030-2035) proves the concepts. The second wave (2035-2040) begins to see improvements in factory efficiency. The third wave (2040+) delivers the promised economics. But the hyperscaler announcements treat wave one as if it were wave three. The Gas Bridge (The Part Nobody Talks About)
On paper, hyperscalers are buying nuclear. In practice, they're buying gas.
While hyperscalers announce nuclear PPAs for 2030-2035, many are quietly signing natural gas power contracts and installing gas turbines at or near data center sites right now.
Amazon, Google, Microsoft - they need power today, not promises for tomorrow. The SMR announcements are for Wall Street analysts and ESG reports. The gas purchases are for operations teams who need to keep servers running.
I'm not criticizing this - it's the rational response to the reality of nuclear timelines. But let's be honest: Nuclear is the long-term vision. Natural gas is the near-term reality.
The risk is that when SMRs inevitably miss their optimistic timelines, public scrutiny will focus on the broken nuclear promises rather than the delivered gas capacity. And nuclear loses credibility again.
The Economics Nobody Mentions
Beyond timelines, there's a cost reality that hyperscaler CFOs understand but marketing materials gloss over.
First-of-a-kind nuclear plants are expensive:
          
      
        
    
These early nuclear builds are landing at $15,000-25,000 per kW - an order of magnitude above what hyperscalers typically pay for generation assets.
Who holds the bag for the $15,000/kW cost?
Three possible answers, none of them comfortable:
1. The hyperscaler (via premium PPA rates): Microsoft, Google, and Amazon can afford to pay above-market rates for the clean energy credit and supply security. But even Big Tech has limits, and their shareholders will eventually ask why they're paying 3-5x market rates for electricity.
2. The utility (via rate-based recovery): Regulated utilities can spread nuclear costs across their entire customer base. This worked for Vogtle, and Georgia ratepayers are now paying for a $35B plant whether they wanted it or not. But it creates political backlash and regulatory risk.
3. The taxpayer (via federal loan guarantees and subsidies): DOE loan programs are backstopping Palisades, TMI, and others. If projects fail or costs balloon, taxpayers absorb the loss. This is industrial policy, not market economics.
The honest answer is usually 'all three' - with the split depending on regulatory structure, political will, and how badly someone wants the clean energy credit. But for a CFO, the question isn't whether nuclear makes long-term sense; it's who's writing the check for the first 10 units while the learning curve plays out.
Why This Matters: The Credibility Crisis Ahead
Nuclear power needs to be part of our clean energy future. The physics works. The safety record is excellent. The energy density is unmatched.
But we are setting up nuclear power to fail - again.
Imagine what happens when:
          
      
        
    
Public sentiment will turn. Politicians will say, 'We tried nuclear, it didn't deliver.' Investors will write off SMR investments. And we'll be back to square one - just like after the 1970s-80s nuclear construction wave failed to deliver on its promises.
The problem isn't nuclear technology. The problem is overpromising and under-delivering on timelines.
The Data Center Power Crisis Is Real. The SMR Solution Is Not (Yet).
If you need power in the next 5 years: Natural gas, restart existing nuclear plants (if you can secure a PPA and financing now), aggressive efficiency measures, and strategic site selection near existing power sources. That's the list.
If you need power in 5-10 years, you might see the first commercial SMRs come online. But plan for delays. Budget for cost overruns. Have backup options.
If you need power in 10-15 years: This is when SMR technology should be genuinely available at scale, assuming the first wave succeeds.
A Former Submarine CO's View on Nuclear Safety and Speed
I ran a nuclear reactor under the ocean for years. The U.S. Navy has operated nuclear vessels for over 60 years with zero reactor accidents causing loss of life or radiation release. We achieved that safety record through rigorous training, conservative procedures, and a culture that prioritizes safety over speed.
When I hear SMR vendors promising 'faster construction' and 'streamlined licensing,' I want to believe them. But I also know that nuclear power is hard. Licensing is thorough for good reasons. First-of-a-kind projects always take longer than predicted. And cutting corners to meet aggressive timelines is how accidents happen.
The answer isn't to slow down nuclear development. The answer is to stop pretending it's faster than it is.
The Bottom Line
Small modular reactors represent genuine innovation and will play a vital role in our energy future. But they won't solve the data center power crisis by 2030.
The current wave of SMR data center announcements is mostly marketing. The timelines are aspirational. The HALEU fuel supply doesn't exist at a commercial scale. The technology isn't failing us. Our timelines and narratives are.
Let's build SMRs. Let's invest in HALEU supply chains. Let's restart existing reactors. Let's make nuclear a cornerstone of clean energy infrastructure.
But let's stop pretending it will happen on the timelines being marketed to hyperscalers today.
Because when nuclear fails to deliver on overpromised timelines, public support evaporates. And we can't afford another lost generation of nuclear development.
Tony Grayson is President & General Manager of Northstar Enterprise + Defense, designing and manufacturing rapid-deployment modular data centers. He previously served as SVP of Physical Infrastructure at Oracle and held senior roles at AWS and Meta. Tony is a former U.S. Navy submarine commander and Stockdale Award winner with a background in nuclear engineering.
Connect with Tony on LinkedIn and at www.tonygraysonvet.com for more insights on AI infrastructure, nuclear power, military leadership lessons, and veteran advocacy.
Did this newsletter challenge your assumptions about nuclear timelines? Please share it with someone in the data center or energy infrastructure. We need honest conversations about nuclear if we want it to succeed.

Did this newsletter challenge your assumptions about nuclear timelines? Please share it with someone in the data center or energy infrastructure. We need honest conversations about nuclear if we want it to succeed.
There is one thing that physics will not do: negotiate with marketing....
Couldn't agree more with your points, and I have been saying the same things. I worked on very fast semiconductor fab construction projects at Intel that went from first-concrete to first-silicon in record time. I also worked at Westinghouse on the Vogtle project that was far over-budget and nowhere close to the project schedule. Nuclear is different. As you said, we need to stop buying into the marketing ploy as nuclear is NOT the short-term solution for the data center needs. We hoped the Vogtle project would lead to the 'nuclear renaissance' but by 2010 it was already unraveling and Westinghouse went bankrupt in 2017. The industry is running down the same path again, and hoping for a different result...insanity.
Fuel is the primary limiting factor: UF6 conversion constraints (impacts the entire industry, 2030 horizon). Most SMRs also intend to use higher enrichment or a different fuel form factor than what’s certified by NRC/facilities available and producing. The NRCs public docket is a good litmus test for timelines
SMR will be trully game changing! But, just one big AI data center will need at least a couple of these. Is this justifiable? Maybe not. AI needs to slow down their horses to be able to grow in a sustainable way, otherwise it will burst. What we are seeing in the USA is an absolute disaster, with some mega AI centers being powered by up to 30 gas turbine generators continuously running, which is basicaly destroying communities. Hope the future is cleaner and greener, and fully believe nuclear reactors and SMR are part of the answer. Also fully believe AI has a place in this world, and can bring immense benefits if developed for purpose and not greed.
